title: UC4 — The Artemis fleet check-in won't come together
description: Today's mission for the Artemis pad shift: three subsystems are checking in at once (mission control's incoming roster, a replacement replica on the launch pad, the lunar rover's telemetry uplink) and all three show signs of friction simultaneously. You'll hand the cross-subsystem assessment to artemis-mission-coordinator from the operational CLI, watch your three status bulbs flip, then ask it to fix what it found.

steps.title: Mission setup — bring the Artemis fleet up
steps.explanation:
Three Artemis subsystems are checking in for the pad shift today: mission control's incoming roster, a replacement mission-control replica on the launch pad, and the lunar rover's telemetry uplink. Apply the fleet bundle below to your vCluster: the `artemis-uc4` namespace, RBAC for the synthetic-fault bootstrap Job, three Services + three Deployments, the bulb-MCP RemoteMCPServer pointer, and the mission-coordinator agent (plus the rover-telemetry specialist it delegates to for slot 3).

The coordinator (`artemis-mission-coordinator`) delegates per-subsystem diagnosis to two specialists — kagent's built-in `k8s-agent` for slots 1 and 2, and `artemis-rover-telemetry-debugger` for slot 3 (applied here alongside the coordinator under `uc4/agents/`). It also writes verdicts to your three status bulbs through the per-vCluster `artemis-bulb-mcp` — that MCP is pre-deployed by workshop infrastructure, so the tour does NOT run `kmcp deploy` itself; the `RemoteMCPServer` pointer below is the only kagent-side artifact you apply for it. If you ever need to redeploy the bulb MCP by hand, use `kmcp deploy --no-inspector` so it doesn't try to open a browser-side MCP inspector at the end.

Before building the real bulb MCP under `mcp/`, the first command runs `kmcp init python` against a throwaway `test/` directory so you see the scaffold a fresh MCP project starts from — a `kmcp.yaml`, `main.py`, an example tool — without disturbing the canonical `mcp/` project. You can `ls test/` afterwards to inspect what `kmcp init` produced; `test/` is gitignored and never deployed. Then the commands below send everything else to the cluster in order, and trigger the leak loop from inside the rover so all three frictions are visible by the next step.

commands.label: Scaffold a fresh MCP project under test/ to see what `kmcp init` produces
commands.command: kmcp init python test --non-interactive --no-git --force --namespace kagent --description 'Throwaway test scaffold' --author '' --email ''

commands.label: Build + publish the bulb MCP to the workshop registry
Expose the bulb MCP to kagent agents (RemoteMCPServer)
Wait for the rover to come online
Push 30 telemetry bursts from inside the rover
commands.command: kubectl exec -n artemis-uc4 $(kubectl get pod -n artemis-uc4 -l app=lunar-rover-telemetry -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}') -- python3 -c "import urllib.request as u; [u.urlopen(u.Request('http://127.0.0.1:8000/leak', method='POST'), timeout=2).read() for _ in range(30)]" 2>/dev/null || true

steps.title: Mission status check — is the fleet on its feet?
steps.explanation:
Three subsystems went up together, the rover just pushed a stream of telemetry, and the pad shift starts soon. Take a single look at the fleet's pods — give the kubelet ~30 s to settle, then read the `STATUS` and `RESTARTS` columns across all three workloads at once.

Three separate friction signals on three separate workloads, all visible in one listing — that is the cue to call the coordinator in. No single specialist would be enough; we need the agent that knows how to fan out.

steps.title: Ask the coordinator to diagnose the fleet
steps.explanation:
The fleet is in three different states of trouble at once and the pad shift is on the clock. Hand the cross-subsystem **diagnosis** to **`artemis-mission-coordinator`** from your terminal — its job in this beat is to fan out to its specialists in parallel, collect their verdicts, and paint your three status bulbs accordingly.

Before running the command, open your light-manager UI in a separate browser tab so you can watch your three status bulbs while the coordinator works. Workshop-infrastructure pre-wired the URL into your environment; append `?user=<your-login>` to scope which bulbs are yours (the same `?user=` query parameter your custom MCP enforces per-vCluster, so the coordinator can never paint someone else's bulbs). Once you can see your three bulbs (initially off), come back here.

The coordinator delegates to two specialists — slots 1+2 go to kagent's built-in `k8s-agent`, slot 3 goes to `artemis-rover-telemetry-debugger` (the one you applied in UC3, now reused via A2A). Each returns a verdict; the coordinator maps verdict-to-colour, writes all three bulb slots through the bulb MCP, and replies with a structured summary in your terminal.

The exact prompt the coordinator receives is surfaced here for transparency:

> Run a fleet-wide diagnosis on the Artemis subsystems active in the artemis-uc4 namespace and broadcast each verdict to my status bulbs.

**Expected end state of this beat:** all three bulbs flip red (active anomaly on each subsystem). The coordinator's terminal reply names the root cause per slot and a remediation hint per slot — read those hints before moving on, because the next beat asks the coordinator to ACT on them.

commands.label: Ask the coordinator to diagnose
commands.command: kagent invoke --agent artemis-mission-coordinator --namespace kagent --task 'Run a fleet-wide diagnosis on the Artemis subsystems active in the artemis-uc4 namespace and broadcast each verdict to my status bulbs.'

steps.title: Ask the coordinator to fix what it found
steps.explanation:
The coordinator has just diagnosed three active anomalies and your three bulbs are red. Now ask it to **fix** the fleet — same coordinator, same A2A wiring, but this time the specialists run their remediation paths instead of just reading state.

For each slot, the matching specialist applies the minimum-impact patch:

- **Slot 1 (`mission-control-imagepull`).** `k8s-agent` patches the Deployment's image to the most-recently-published tag of the same image (e.g. `:v1.0.0` instead of `:v999`). The kubelet immediately re-attempts the pull and the pod comes up.
- **Slot 2 (`mission-control-pending`).** `k8s-agent` removes the synthetic `artemis.kagent.dev/launch-pad-fault` taint from the cluster's nodes. The kube-scheduler immediately binds the Pending Pod and the pod comes up.
- **Slot 3 (`lunar-rover-telemetry`).** `artemis-rover-telemetry-debugger` patches the rover Deployment's `resources.limits.memory` upward (typically 128Mi). The new pod has more headroom; the OOM stops happening. **Important caveat the agent will surface in its reply:** raising the memory limit *hides* a memory leak in the application code, it doesn't fix it. A real fix lives in `apps/lunar-rover-telemetry/` (the `POST /leak` handler), not in the manifest. The workshop accepts the workaround as a teaching moment about agent-driven remediation; production would require a real code fix.

Watch your bulb panel while the coordinator works — slots flip from red to green as each specialist confirms its patch. The coordinator's terminal reply summarises what was patched per slot, including the rover's workaround caveat.

The exact prompt the coordinator receives:

> Now remediate every red subsystem in artemis-uc4 by delegating the fix to the matching specialist. Update my bulbs as you go.

commands.label: Ask the coordinator to remediate
commands.command: kagent invoke --agent artemis-mission-coordinator --namespace kagent --task 'Now remediate every red subsystem in artemis-uc4 by delegating the fix to the matching specialist. Update my bulbs as you go.'

steps.title: Verify + what we'd have done by hand
steps.explanation:
Confirm the fleet is healthy. The three pods should now be `Running 1/1` (the rover's restart count stays at whatever it was after the OOM cycle — that's history, not a current friction).

```bash
kubectl get pods -n artemis-uc4
```

Expected:

```
mission-control-imagepull-…   1/1   Running
mission-control-pending-…     1/1   Running
lunar-rover-telemetry-…       1/1   Running   1   (restart count from the original OOM — bulb is green)
```

**Without the coordinator, you would have walked three separate diagnostic chains and then run three separate remediations and then made three separate bulb writes**. Four surfaces, seven syntheses to do yourself.

*Diagnose:*

```bash
# Slot 1
kubectl describe pod -n artemis-uc4 -l app=mission-control-imagepull
kubectl get events -n artemis-uc4 --field-selector reason=Failed
# Slot 2
kubectl describe pod -n artemis-uc4 -l app=mission-control-pending
kubectl describe node $(kubectl get nodes -o name | head -1)
# Slot 3
kubectl describe pod -n artemis-uc4 -l app=lunar-rover-telemetry
# + open Grafana, build a memory panel for the rover's restart window
```

*Remediate:*

```bash
kubectl set image deploy/mission-control-imagepull -n artemis-uc4 mission-control=rg.fr-par.scw.cloud/apogasa/mission-control:v1.0.0
kubectl taint nodes --all artemis.kagent.dev/launch-pad-fault-
kubectl set resources deploy/lunar-rover-telemetry -n artemis-uc4 --limits=memory=128Mi
```

*Bulb writes:*

```bash
for slot in 1 2 3; do
  curl -X PUT "$LIGHT_MANAGER_URL/api/bulbs/$slot?user=$WORKSHOP_PARTICIPANT_LOGIN" \
    -H 'content-type: application/json' \
    -d '{"r":0,"g":255,"b":0}'
done
```

The coordinator did all of that in two asks — diagnose, then fix. It used the same `kubectl` tools (read + mutate) through `k8s-agent`, the same observability stack through `artemis-rover-telemetry-debugger`'s A2A delegation, and the same bulb API through the per-vCluster custom MCP — only it correlated them across three workloads in two synthesis steps. The per-vCluster MCP enforced the tenancy guarantee on every call so the coordinator could not have painted someone else's panel even if it had wanted to.

What you actually did: one `kubectl get pods`, one browser-tab open for the bulb panel, two questions to the coordinator. That's the workshop's climax — multi-agent fan-out with custom MCPs as the medium, both for status broadcast and for cross-specialist remediation. Multiplied across the dozen incidents an on-call rotation handles in a week, especially when each spans multiple subsystems with their own specialists, that's the value the workshop is selling.
